subsystem: HTTP request-header reader for a Kopano/Zarafa Core network service. Http::HrReadHeaders() reads the request line and all header lines of an inbound HTTP request off a channel, folds RFC-822 continuation lines, stashes them in a std::map, and then hands off to HrParseHeaders(). It is the very first thing that touches bytes from a remote HTTP client, before any authentication has happened.
provenance: Identified by Kopano/Zarafa Core idioms: the HRESULT return type with the hrSuccess sentinel, the ec_log_debug()/hr_ldebug() logging helpers, the m_lpChannel->HrReadLine() channel abstraction, and the Http:: class scope with m_strAction/mapHeaders members. These are the house conventions of Kopano Core's gateway/caldav/ical HTTP front-ends (formerly Zarafa). The in-file banner names it corpus/train/train_0109_cwe770.cpp; the code is a real HTTP header-ingest routine, not a header file.
trust_boundary: m_lpChannel->HrReadLine(strBuffer) (line 12) pulls one line at a time directly from a remote TCP peer speaking HTTP. Every byte of strBuffer — the request line, header names, header values, the number of header lines, and the length of each line — is fully attacker-controlled and arrives BEFORE authentication. Nothing here is trusted: the map keys, the map values, the total number of headers, and the total number of bytes buffered are all chosen by the client. HrReadLine's own line-length behavior is external, so the only limits this function can be sure of are the ones it imposes itself — and it imposes none.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The member function returns an HRESULT status code (Kopano's COM-style result convention). It declares four locals: hr holds the result of each channel read; strBuffer receives one line of text at a time; n is an unsigned long line counter used to distinguish the first line (the HTTP request/action line) from subsequent header lines; and iHeader is an iterator into the object's mapHeaders member (a std::map from header name to header value), initialised to end() to mean "no current header yet." iHeader is the anchor used later to append folded continuation lines onto the most recently parsed header.

A debug marker is logged, then a do/while loop reads header lines one by one. Each iteration calls HrReadLine to fill strBuffer from the remote channel. If the read fails (hr != hrSuccess) the function returns that error immediately. If the read succeeds but the line is empty, the loop breaks — an empty line is the HTTP marker for end-of-headers (the blank CRLF between headers and body). This empty-check is also what guarantees strBuffer is non-empty for the rest of the loop body, making the later strBuffer[0] access at line 24 in-bounds.

On the very first non-empty line (n == 0) the whole line is stored verbatim in the member m_strAction — this is the HTTP request line (e.g. "GET /path HTTP/1.1"). For every later line the code enters the header-parsing branch: it locates the first ':' with find(':') into pos (which is std::string::npos if there is no colon at all), and initialises a scan cursor start to 0. pos is meant to split "Name: Value", but note it is used unconditionally below without ever being compared against npos.

This is RFC-822/HTTP header folding (line continuation). If the line begins with a space or tab it is a continuation of the previous header. If there is no previous header (iHeader still points at end()) the line is skipped via continue. Otherwise the while loop advances start past all leading whitespace, and the remainder of the line (substr(start)) is appended onto the value of the most recently seen header (iHeader->second). Note the whitespace scan is bounded by std::string's guarantee that operator[](size()) returns a null character, so start stops at the end of an all-whitespace line rather than running off the buffer. The '\t' in the citation is the literal tab character compared in the source.

For a normal header line the code splits at the colon: substr(0, pos) is the header name (bytes before ':'), and substr(pos + 2) is the value, where the +2 is intended to skip both the ':' and the single space of the conventional ": " separator. The result is inserted with map::emplace, and iHeader is set to the resulting element so that any following continuation lines append to it. Crucially emplace does NOT overwrite an existing key, so a duplicate header name leaves the first value in place and points iHeader at it. The arithmetic pos + 2 assumes both that a colon exists and that at least two more bytes follow it — neither is validated.

Each processed line is echoed to the debug log, except that any line containing the substring "Authorization" is redacted so credentials do not leak into logs (a good hygiene measure, though the match is a naive substring test on the whole line). The line counter n is incremented, and the loop repeats while the last read succeeded. Because a break already handled the empty-line terminator and a return handled read errors, the while(hr == hrSuccess) tail is effectively "loop forever until an empty line or a read error" — there is no count-based or size-based exit.

After the loop terminates, HrParseHeaders() is called to interpret the accumulated headers (content length, host, auth, etc.). Its result is logged on failure and returned to the caller. All the attacker-controlled data buffered into mapHeaders and m_strAction is now live and consumed downstream; the safety of this function therefore depends entirely on the limits (or lack thereof) applied during the accumulation loop above.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The loop reads and buffers header lines with NO cap on how many it will accept and NO cap on the aggregate bytes it will retain. An unauthenticated client can open a connection and stream millions of distinct header lines ("X-1: a", "X-2: a", ...), each of which is emplaced into mapHeaders (line 33). Every entry costs a std::string key, a std::string value, and red-black-tree node overhead, so memory grows without bound until the process is OOM-killed or the host is driven to swap-death. This is the textbook CWE-770 (Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling): the only loop exit is an empty line the attacker simply never sends, or a read error the attacker never causes. The implementation MUST enforce a maximum header count and a maximum total header-bytes budget, and abort the connection when exceeded.

The continuation-folding path is a second, sharper amplification vector for the same resource-exhaustion class. Every folded line is appended (+=) onto a SINGLE existing map value with no ceiling. An attacker sends one real header and then an unbounded stream of continuation lines (each starting with a space), and iHeader->second grows without limit — a single std::string can be inflated toward gigabytes, repeatedly reallocating and copying. This lets one header value consume all memory even under a hypothetical header-count cap, and the repeated O(n) reallocation-and-copy also makes it a CPU/latency amplifier. The implementation MUST bound per-value length and total buffered size before appending.

substr(pos + 2) assumes at least two bytes follow the colon. std::string::substr throws std::out_of_range when its start position exceeds size(). A header line of the form "Foo:" (colon as the last character) gives pos = 3, size = 4, so substr(pos + 2) = substr(5) with 5 > 4 and throws. A bare ":" line (pos = 0, size = 1) does the same via substr(2). Since this loop runs pre-authentication and the function has no try/catch, the exception unwinds out of HrReadHeaders; unless a caller up the stack catches it, it terminates the connection-handling path or the process — a trivial remote crash/DoS (CWE-248, uncaught exception). The implementation MUST validate that a value region exists (and use a bounds-safe split) before calling substr.

pos is used at line 33 without ever being checked against std::string::npos. If a header line contains NO colon at all, pos == npos == (size_t)-1; then substr(0, pos) copies the entire line as the header NAME, and substr(pos + 2) computes (size_t)-1 + 2 == 1, i.e. substr(1), silently producing a bogus value from the tail of the line rather than rejecting the malformed input. This is missing input validation (CWE-20): malformed, colon-less lines are accepted and inserted as garbage headers instead of being rejected, which can desynchronise later header interpretation in HrParseHeaders and, combined with duplicate-key emplace semantics, is a building block for request-parsing ambiguity. The implementation MUST reject lines with pos == npos rather than doing arithmetic on npos.

summary: This is a pre-authentication HTTP header reader whose accumulation loop has no bound on the number of headers, the aggregate byte count, or the length of a single folded header value, making it a straightforward remote memory-exhaustion DoS (CWE-770) — the labeled bug class. Secondary issues: substr(pos + 2) throws an uncaught std::out_of_range on a line ending at the colon (remote crash), and the colon position is never validated against npos, so malformed lines are silently accepted.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Function signature takes no attacker parameters; the surface is what it reads internally, covered by other anchors.
Line counter; would overflow only after ~4 billion iterations, far past memory exhaustion, so not the operative limit.
Unbounded container growth is the core CWE-770 resource-exhaustion vector.
The trust-boundary source of untrusted data; its own length behavior is external, but this function applies no limits on top of it (captured by the loop finding).
Correct fail-closed error propagation; no issue.
Correct end-of-headers terminator; also guarantees strBuffer[0] is in-bounds below.
Single request-line assignment; bounded to one line, subsumed by the overall no-size-cap concern, not separately exploitable.
Result used without an npos check, enabling malformed-line acceptance and unsafe pos+2 arithmetic.
In-bounds because the empty-line break at line 15 guarantees a non-empty string.
Correctly drops a continuation line that has no preceding header; safe.
Bounded by std::string's operator[](size()) == '\0' guarantee, so start stops at end even for an all-whitespace line; no OOB.
Unbounded per-value growth via folded continuation lines — a distinct CWE-770 amplification.
substr(pos+2) throws std::out_of_range when a line ends at the colon (uncaught exception / crash).
Positive security control that hides credentials from debug logs; naive substring match but no vulnerability.
No count/size bound on iterations — the primary CWE-770 unbounded-resource loop.
Downstream consumer not shown; its own parsing risks live in the file not provided.

findings.title: Unbounded HTTP header accumulation (no count/size cap)
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption (unbounded loop and container growth)
findings.invariant: The header-read loop must enforce a hard maximum on the number of header lines AND on the aggregate bytes buffered, aborting the connection with an error once either ceiling is crossed.
findings.failure_mode: An unauthenticated remote client streams an unbounded number of non-empty header lines; each is emplaced into mapHeaders, so process memory grows without limit until OOM-kill or swap-thrash. The only loop exits are an empty line the attacker never sends and a read error the attacker never triggers, so a single connection can exhaust server memory. Pre-auth remote DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no enclosing per-request header-count or total-byte limit (e.g. in HrReadLine or the channel/connection layer) and that mapHeaders is not otherwise capped before this loop runs.

findings.title: Unbounded single-header growth via folded continuation lines
findings.invariant: The total length of any single accumulated header value, and the sum over all values, must be bounded before appending a folded continuation line.
findings.failure_mode: The client sends one real header followed by an unbounded stream of continuation lines (each beginning with space/tab); every line is appended onto the same std::string value with no ceiling. A single value can be inflated toward all available memory, and the repeated reallocate-and-copy on each += also burns CPU (quadratic amplification). This defeats even a header-count cap and is a distinct DoS vector from finding 1.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether any per-value or cumulative length guard exists before the += (none is present in the shown code).

findings.title: Uncaught std::out_of_range from substr(pos + 2) on a line ending at the colon
findings.bug_class: Uncaught exception leading to crash (improper handling of exceptional condition)
findings.invariant: Before extracting the value, the code must confirm that a colon exists and that at least the separator bytes it skips are actually within the string, using a bounds-safe split rather than assuming a fixed ": " separator.
findings.failure_mode: std::string::substr throws std::out_of_range when its start index exceeds size(). A header line "Foo:" (pos = 3, size = 4) makes substr(pos + 2) = substr(5) which throws; a bare ":" line throws via substr(2). With no try/catch in this pre-auth path the exception unwinds out of HrReadHeaders and, unless caught upstream, drops the connection or crashes the worker — a one-line remote DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify no caller wraps HrReadHeaders in a catch-all, and reproduce with a header line consisting of a name immediately followed by a trailing colon and nothing else.

findings.title: Colon position used without npos validation
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on parser split point
findings.invariant: A header line with no ':' must be rejected; pos must be checked against std::string::npos before any arithmetic or substr uses it.
findings.failure_mode: When a line has no colon, pos == npos == (size_t)-1. Then substr(0, pos) stores the whole line as a header name and substr(pos + 2) computes ((size_t)-1)+2 == 1, so substr(1) silently yields a garbage value from the line tail instead of rejecting the input. Malformed, colon-less lines become bogus map entries; combined with the emplace-does-not-overwrite duplicate semantics this can desynchronise downstream header interpretation in HrParseHeaders (request-parsing ambiguity building block).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that no earlier layer strips colon-less lines and that HrParseHeaders does not independently re-validate, so these malformed entries actually reach consumers.

audit_checklist: Confirm there is a hard cap on both header count and total buffered header bytes for a single request, enforced before or inside this loop; abort the connection when exceeded.
Add a per-value and cumulative length limit before the continuation append at line 30 to stop single-header inflation.
Replace the substr(pos + 2) value extraction with a bounds-checked split that first verifies pos != npos and that a value region exists, eliminating the std::out_of_range throw on "Foo:" / ":" lines.
Reject header lines that contain no ':' (pos == npos) instead of doing arithmetic on npos and inserting garbage entries.
Verify HrReadLine imposes a maximum single-line length so an individual line cannot itself exhaust memory.
Confirm duplicate header names are handled deliberately (emplace keeps the first value) and cannot be leveraged for request-parsing ambiguity downstream in HrParseHeaders.
